result:
The first thing I notice when I wake up is the weight of the air—how it presses against my skin like a second, denser skin, as though the night has left behind a residue of dreams that hasn’t decided whether to evaporate. I lie still and let the room assemble itself out of darkness: the ceiling fan with its hesitant orbit, the dresser that lost one brass knob in a move I can’t date precisely, the stack of books I keep meaning to alphabetize but never do because I enjoy the jagged skyline they make against the wall. I like how objects reveal themselves in the half-light, like actors who refuse to bow until the audience has forgotten the play has started.  

Outside, the city is already practicing its monologue of buses, garbage trucks, and a lone leaf blower that sounds like a bee the size of a refrigerator. Somewhere a car alarm goes off, the same four-note motif it has been broadcasting since 1997, a tiny civic anthem. I imagine the car hoarse with age, its red light blinking like a weary eye. I used to fume at that sound—proof of malfunction, of entropy, of our collective inability to keep anything running the way the manual promised—but now I find it oddly comforting, a reminder that even machines grow old and keep shouting their names into the void.  

I get up, shuffle to the kitchen, and perform the small liturgy of coffee: the squat black kettle, the paper filter that blooms like a chrysanthemum when the water hits it, the first hiss that always reminds me of a bicycle tire losing pressure. While the water climbs toward 200 degrees Fahrenheit, I stand at the window and watch the neighbor’s cat—an orange tabby named either Marmalade or Tomorrow, depending which kid you ask—balance on the fence rail. The cat’s tail is a question mark posed against the morning. I think about how cats experience time: not as a straight line but as a series of warm patches and possible ambushes, a map of opportunities rather than obligations. The cat blinks at me once, slowly, granting absolution for every deadline I’ve ever missed.  

Coffee ready, I sit at the table with the wobble that I’ve corrected three times by folding a gas-station loyalty card under the shortest leg. I open my notebook to a blank page and wait for the first sentence to arrive. People think writing is a matter of having something to say, but mostly it is a matter of listening for what wants to be said, a dog-whistle pitched above the frequency of intention. Some mornings the sentence arrives wearing combat boots; other mornings it tiptoes in wearing the socks my grandmother used to darn, the ones with the tiny irises along the cuff. Today it arrives in the form of a question: What if every place you’ve ever loved is still occurring somewhere, like a radio station you can no longer receive?  

I let the question settle into the paper and feel it build its small campfire. I remember the hallway of my elementary school that smelled equally of floor wax and sour milk, how the fluorescent lights flickered at 60 hertz, a frequency that always made me feel I was walking inside someone's headache. I remember the creek behind the rented house where I lived at twenty-five, its water the color of root beer except where broken glass caught the sun and threw green sparks against the bank. I remember the subway platform at Canal Street in 2003, how the oncoming train pushed a wind that smelled of electricity and rat poison and possibility, how a stranger in a Mets cap handed me a tiny plastic dinosaur and said, “Take him home, he’s tired.” I still have the dinosaur; he stands on my desk wearing a smudge of permanent melancholy.  

Each of those places is gone the way a wave is gone—no longer visible yet still somewhere traveling. I picture them at this very instant: the hallway might be a call center now, the creek might be culverted beneath a parking lot, the subway platform might be renovated into stainless steel glare. Yet they persist inside me like Russian dolls carved from bone. I carry them without meaning to, the way I carry the scar on my knee from a bicycle crash age eight, the way I carry the exact timbre of my mother’s laugh before the chemo roughened her throat. Memory is not a vault but a companion; it walks beside me wearing the faces of rooms and streets and scraps of sky.  

I refill my cup and notice the coffee is cooling faster than usual. Autumn is leaning against the windowpane, exhaling its metallic scent of rust and departure. I think about how seasons used to arrive with ceremony—trucks selling pumpkins, pharmacies hanging inflatable snowmen—but now they slip in like houseguests who have secretly copied the key. I realize I am forty-one, an age that refuses to sound real no matter how many times I say it aloud. Forty-one is the number of kilometers a marathoner dodges cramps to finish, the number of symphonies Mozart did not live to write, the number of stairs in the tower of the small church where I once sang tenor though I could never read the notes.  

I read somewhere that the human heart beats about 2.5 billion times in an average life. I do the crude arithmetic: I’ve spent roughly 1.2 billion already, a fortune dwindling. Each beat a coin flipped into a fountain. I think about the moments I wasted being bored: standing in post-office lines, scrolling the glowing rectangles, sitting in meetings where agendas reproduced like fruit flies. Yet even boredom is a flavor of being alive, the way plain gelatin still counts as food. I resolve—once again—to be less stingy with my remaining beats, knowing I will break the promise by sundown. Promises to the self are helium balloons; they float upward and get stuck on bedroom ceilings, still bright but no longer reachable.  

The cat appears at the back door, whiskers striped like prison bars. I let it in and it strolls across my pages, leaving a constellation of paw prints that blur the ink. I find I don’t mind; the prints look like a map of an archipelago I might one day visit, islands where sentences run freely and no one asks for credentials. The cat head-butts my elbow, a reminder that creatures other than sentences demand affection. I scratch beneath its chin and feel the engine of its purr start, a small motor that somehow burns nothing.  

I think about how animals experience death without calendar. The cat doesn’t know it will die, yet sometimes it stares into the corner of the room as though watching a shape I can’t see. Maybe it senses the approaching moment the way we sense barometric drop in our joints. Or maybe it simply stares at dust. Either way, the result is the same: a pause, a tilt of the ears, then back to the business of being alive, which mostly involves finding the one sunbeam on the floor and napping until it moves.  

I wish I could nap like that, but guilt has always been my metronome. Even on vacation I time the intervals between pleasures, making sure I’m not enjoying myself too much, as though joy were a natural resource regulated by a federal agency. I’ve started therapy to unlearn this, but therapy itself requires appointments, co-pays, the willingness to sit in a beige room and discuss whether my childhood was “good enough” or merely “sufficient.” My therapist has aquariums in her office; I count the neon tetras when the questions get too sharp. Last week she asked what I would do if I could subtract my guilt for one afternoon. I said I would walk into the ocean until the water reached my pockets, then turn around and walk back, letting gravity do the work of removing everything that wasn’t me. She nodded as though this were a feasible prescription.  

Outside, clouds shift across the sun, turning the room into a page someone erased incompletely. I think about how light is both particle and wave, how it arrives in packets of yes or no, on or off, yet also in swells like music. Maybe memory is similar, quantum in nature: both the thing and the echo of the thing, depending on who observes. I picture my grandmother’s kitchen existing in superposition—both gone and present, the yellow wall clock with its loud tick still measuring a minute that ended decades ago. When I visited her I would sit at the Formica table and draw on the backs of grocery receipts. She hummed hymns while kneading dough, her wedding ring clicking against the bowl. I remember the smell of yeast rising like a slow applause. I remember how she never threw away bread heels; she saved them in the freezer “for stuffing,” though she never made stuffing. After she died we found a bread-bag mountain in the freezer, each twist-tied label in her tidy Catholic-school cursive: 1984, 1985, 1986. She was saving time in the form of starch, a pension plan against hunger that never arrived.  

I wonder what I’m accumulating in the freezer of my own life. Frozen conversations I never rehearsed? The last voicemail from my father, which I’ve kept but can’t bear to replay? I imagine the electrons holding his voice intact, a tiny galaxy spinning inside my phone. One day the phone will fall to the bottom of a drawer, battery swollen like a sick liver, and eventually someone will recycle it without knowing that a piece of someone’s lungs still lives in there. I picture the recyclers in Guangdong or Nairobi, workers with screwdrivers and masks, extracting gold from circuit boards, inadvertently releasing the sound of my father saying “Talk to you soon, kiddo,” into the air of a continent he never visited.  

I close the notebook; the cat has fallen asleep on the open page, claiming another country. I decide to go for a walk, though I have nowhere to go. I put on the sneakers with the hole in the toe because they are the most honest ones I own. The street outside is lined with maples whose leaves have started to burn themselves into cautionary orange. A single skateboarder coasts past, wheels sounding like applause from a very small audience. I nod; he ignores me, which feels correct.  

I pass the Methodist church that has replaced its hymn board with an LED sign scrolling feel-good aphorisms: YOU ARE LOVED. GOD IS BIGGER. HAVE A BLESSED DAY. I wonder what the prophets would make of autocue scripture. Inside the stained-glass windows glow with primary-color stories I once believed were historical documentaries. Now I see them as postcards from a country that issues visas selectively. Still, I pause at the image of Thomas touching the wound, his hand extended not quite far enough to reach, a moment suspended between doubt and relief. I realize every story I love ends in that gap—between the finger and the flesh, the promise and the proof.  

I keep walking until I reach the river that bisects the city like a sentence interrupted by a long dash. The water today is brown and quiet, ferrying occasional Styrofoam artifacts and the reflection of a hawk tracing circles in the sky. I sit on a bench graffitied with initials inside hearts, declarations that someone was here with someone else and believed permanence could be spray-painted. A jogger passes wearing earbuds, breathing in perfect four-four time. I try to imagine her soundtrack—maybe Beyoncé, maybe an audiobook about habits of successful entrepreneurs. Whatever it is, it has turned her body into a metronome, and I feel a pang of envy for people who can outsource the keeping of time.  

The hawk dives and emerges with something small and writhing. I look away but not fast enough. The brutality of the world is also a kind of timekeeping: every creature has a certain number of beats before another creature claims them. I once dated a woman who could not stomach nature documentaries; she said they were pornographic in their honesty. We broke up after she saw me swat a mosquito with more enthusiasm than the situation required. She said I was too eager to kill. I said I was too eager to sleep without itching. Underneath both statements was the larger question of whether we were willing to let the world bite us or whether we insisted on biting first.  

I check my phone—no notifications, just the time glaring like a disappointed parent. I have been gone forty-three minutes; the cat will be wondering where the warmth went. I stand to head back and notice a single glove on the ground, palm up, as though imploring coins. I pick it up: navy wool, thumb frayed, small enough to belong to a child. I picture the mother discovering the loss, the tears on the walk home, the story that will be told for weeks: “Remember when you dropped your glove and thought the river had stolen it?” I hang the glove on the railing like a flag of surrender, though I’m not sure which side is surrendering.  

Walking home, I pass a house with wind chimes that sound exactly like the theme song of a sitcom I watched in the nineties. Nostalgia is a mugger hiding in plain sight; it knocks you down and then helps you up apologizing. I hum along, though I can’t name the show. Maybe it never existed; maybe the chimes are simply tuned to a chord that unlocks the part of the brain storing laugh tracks. Either way, I arrive at my porch lighter, as though someone removed coins from my pockets while I wasn’t looking.  

Inside, the cat greets me with indifference engineered to perfection. I boil water for tea this time, wanting a different ceremony. While the kettle journeys toward its whistle, I open a drawer and find the dinosaur the stranger gave me twenty years ago. He is still tired, or maybe I am projecting. I stand him on the windowsill facing the backyard, where the last of the sun fingers through the fence slats. I imagine him telling the cat stories of the underground: the rumble, the sparks, the way darkness moves aside when enough speed is applied. The cat blinks, unimpressed.  

I sit and reopen the notebook. The paw-print islands have dried into faint topographies. I write: “Today I walked until the world showed me its tooth, then its tongue, then its missing glove.” I write: “Memory is not the opposite of forgetting but its accomplice.” I write: “Tomorrow I will wake to the same weight of air, the same fan with its planetary wobble, the same question of whether to keep listening or start speaking.” I write until the page looks like a forest at night, all vertical strokes and small gaps where light might escape.  

Eventually the kettle whistles, the cat yawns, dusk folds the room into a paper airplane and launches it toward evening. Somewhere a car alarm starts the same four-note lament. Somewhere a bread heel waits in a freezer to become part of a meal that may never be served. Somewhere my father’s voice still says “Talk to you soon,” traveling through copper wires that no longer exist. I close the notebook and decide that 2500 words is not a finish line but a mile marker on a road that keeps unspooling. Outside, the streetlights come on like someone slowly gaining confidence. I stand at the window and watch moths arrive, tiny pilgrims following an invisible compass. They beat their wings against the bulb, not knowing it is not the moon. I think: neither do we, and yet we keep navigating by whatever light refuses to switch off.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
